rationale: This reaction is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction. Tert-butanol is protonated by sulfuric acid and loses water to form the stable tert-butyl carbocation. This carbocation is then trapped by the nucleophilic nitrogen of acetonitrile, forming a nitrilium ion intermediate. Subsequent hydrolysis yields N-tert-butylacetamide. All reactants and products are simple, acyclic, and RDKit-parseable, clearly illustrating the core transformation of a carbocation source and a nitrile into an N-alkyl amide.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic, saturated aliphatic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with other named reactions. It avoids arenes (Friedel-Crafts), alkenes (Markovnikov addition, Prins), carbonyls (Beckmann rearrangement), or other complex functional groups that could cause collisions with unrelated reaction families. The transformation is a clear C-N bond formation to create an amide, which is the defining feature of the Ritter reaction.
